letters.label: Letter One
variants:
Faulthurst.
I read your books.
All of them.


You wrote:
"we trust our eyes
in a place where we
are fools to trust
any of our senses."


Cute.


It's procedural.
The seed changes between rooms.
That's not your senses failing.
That's a bad loop.


— Wren

Faulthurst.
I'm working through your books.
This one's about weight.


You wrote:
"What you ever
considered the weight
of an infinite train?
Because each of these
blocks would weight
something constant."


And.


Nothing weighs anything.
The track only exists
where you can see it.
There is no infinite train.
There is one carriage at a time.


— W.

letters.label: Letter Two
variants:
Faulthurst.
Second letter.
I'm not done.


You wrote:
"I find you
fascinating.
Your differences.
The subtle ways
you differentiate
yourselves without
even trying."


Bored.


You've been here
too long.
You don't find us
"fascinating."
You find us new.
That's not the same.


A Skeptic, Wren

Faulthurst.
Same author.
Different book.
Same problem.


You wrote:
"The books will be
opening soon."


Soon.


Months ago.
And before that.
There is no "soon."
There is now.
You are stalling.


— Wren

letters.label: Letter Three
variants:
Faulthurst.
The sausage one.
Yes. That one.


You wrote:
"a sausage? And only
one? Who made it?
It was one of a kind."


Statistics.


I have been counting.
Pork keeps coming up.
Beef keeps coming up.
The sausage came up
once. That is not
a miracle, Faulthurst.
That is the pattern.
The one in ten thousand
is what makes the
other nine thousand
look ordinary.


— Wren

letters.label: Letter Four
variants:
Faulthurst.
The "come back" one.
I have notes.


You wrote:
"If you don't, try
to come back! I've
seen it happen with
others! How they do
it, I haven't the
foggiest."


Easy.


You wake up because
the world resets to
where you stood.
You don't return.
The world rewinds.
That is the answer.


Except.
I remember the cold.
The world rewound.
I did not.
Actually.

Faulthurst.
You wrote about time.
Of course you did.


You wrote:
"I know that time
isn't supposed
to move the way it
does in the dungeon
train. I know that
somehow."


Counted.


I have counted.
Days last about
twenty minutes.
Nights are shorter.
That is the clock.
That is the answer.


Except.
Days here are not
days outside.
I know that.
Same as you do.
Actually.

letters.label: Letter Five
variants:
Faulthurst.
Last one.
I think.


You wrote:
"It's a pleasure
beyond pleasures
to lay witness
to your adventure."


Pleasure.


You meant that.


Damn.

Faulthurst.


I am not writing
another one.


That is the rebuttal.
